sample.title: SDG Progress Intelligence Report
visuals: Executive Kpi Cards: Targets on track
Regional Heat Map: Equity gap index
sample.promise: A decision-ready sustainable development publication for Africa, built from traceable synthetic evidence.
sample.executive_story: What follows is a governed account of sustainable development performance in Africa: where it is working, where it is not, and what to do next. The evidence identifies uneven progress, concentrated risk, and a set of opportunities still open to act on. Every finding traces to a synthetic source record, every recommendation names an accountable owner, and every statistic in this publication is drawn from a single, internally consistent dataset.

report.subtitle: Decision intelligence for Sustainable Development

branding.tagline: Every Voice. Every Language. Every Insight.
branding.synthetic_notice: Synthetic Demonstration Publication produced by VoiceInsights Africa. All people, quotations, locations and statistics are synthetic, internally governed demonstration content and are not official statistics.

report.executive_summary: What follows is a governed account of sustainable development performance in Africa: where it is working, where it is not, and what to do next. The evidence identifies uneven progress, concentrated risk, and a set of opportunities still open to act on. Every finding traces to a synthetic source record, every recommendation names an accountable owner, and every statistic in this publication is drawn from a single, internally consistent dataset.

executive_book.executive_brief: The priority for the UN coordination team is converting early sustainable development progress into durable gains before the next reporting cycle.

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Focus acceleration plans on off-track targets.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: The evidence points toward improved sustainable development outcomes, though the size of the gain will depend on how consistently the action is resourced.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Likely — though not certain — erosion of momentum if the initial sponsor changes role
dependencies: Timely data collection for the next reporting cycle
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Medium — likely fundable from existing lines, pending confirmation
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Medium — likely fundable from existing lines, pending confirmation
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Milestone completion rate tracked against the DEC-SDG-01 delivery plan
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: Verified evidence of at least 80% delivery against the agreed milestone plan

decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: Reduced variation in sustainable development delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Fragmented accountability once implementation moves beyond the pilot stage
dependencies: Sign-off from the relevant oversight body
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Medium to high — requires a dedicated budget line and phased release
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Medium to high — requires a dedicated budget line and phased release
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Proportion of agreed implementation steps verified on schedule for DEC-SDG-02
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: At least 80% of milestones achieved with verified evidence

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Align budget tagging with SDG contribution logic.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: A stronger, more defensible case for sustainable development investment in the next planning cycle.
dependencies: Stable staffing through the delivery window
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Low — absorbable within existing allocations, subject to confirmation
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Low — absorbable within existing allocations, subject to confirmation
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Quarterly-verified progress against the milestones agreed for this high-tier decision
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: A majority of milestones achieved, each independently verified against real evidence

critical_findings.title: Target trajectory
critical_findings.text: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.
critical_findings.interpretation: This reading is more strongly supported by some evidence types than others — treat it as directionally reliable rather than precisely measured.
critical_findings.headline: Target trajectory is the binding constraint

critical_findings.title: Leave-no-one-behind gaps
critical_findings.text: What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
critical_findings.interpretation: Consistent across quantitative and qualitative sources, and most pronounced in lower-performing locations.
critical_findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the leave-no-one-behind gaps gap

critical_findings.title: Financing alignment
critical_findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
critical_findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
critical_findings.headline: Financing alignment gains remain exposed to reversal

critical_risks.risk: Leadership turnover during implementation stalls momentum on sustainable development
Evidence and monitoring capacity lags the pace of decisions being made on sustainable development

top_opportunities: Evidence-led reallocation of resources already committed to sustainable development
Scaling what is already working in the highest-performing region
Stronger feedback accountability between frontline delivery and leadership

executive_book.budget_implications: Most of the indicated action in sustainable development is fundable by reprioritizing existing lines; the highest-tier decisions may still need a dedicated allocation.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: Delaying action on sustainable development performance is unlikely to be neutral: the evidence suggests the current gap compounds rather than holds steady.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence in this synthetic demonstration is strong throughout; still not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: A 12–18 month improvement path for sustainable development is realistic, provided named owners and quarterly evidence review stay in place.
key_messages: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.

What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Two things are true at once about data availability. The national picture is improving, and Central Africa — where frontline workers report a 86% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Sustainable development performance in Africa looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once data availability is disaggregated. Central Africa anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.

54%. That is the climate-development co-benefits signal recorded in East Africa, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, low-income households sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in East Africa, concentrated among low-income households. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

evidence.evidence_chain: Dataset → Question Q1 → Respondent group → Finding 1 → Decision 1
evidence.survey_question: Q1: How does target trajectory affect sustainable development outcomes in East Africa?
evidence.quote: “Why does East Africa lag behind? Ask rural women, and the answer comes back to target trajectory.”
evidence.transcript_excerpt: Synthetic transcript 1: participants connected target trajectory to uneven delivery, accountability and the need for locally owned follow-up.

statistical_intelligence.sampling_frame: Synthetic frame covering programme-relevant population groups in Africa
statistical_intelligence.weighting: Post-stratification weights normalized to the synthetic population frame
statistical_intelligence.confidence_intervals: 95% confidence intervals shown for eligible weighted estimates
statistical_intelligence.regression: Multivariable regression included where outcome and assumptions are suitable

missing_data_analysis.treatment: multiple imputation for eligible analytical variables; complete-case sensitivity check

statistical_intelligence.data_quality_assessment: Passed synthetic consistency, range, duplicate and contradiction checks
statistical_intelligence.reproducibility_notes: Deterministic generator, documented transformations and export manifest
methodological_limitations: Synthetic data cannot support population inference

research_objectives: Assess sustainable development performance and equity
Identify operational drivers and decision priorities
evaluation_questions: Which actions are most likely to improve performance?
methodology.sampling_frame: Synthetic programme population frame for Africa
methodology.metadata: Complete synthetic data dictionary and lineage registry

limitations: All content is synthetic and must not be interpreted as official statistics
Budget implications are directional and require formal costing
Causal claims are not made without an appropriate design

executive_commentary.text: For Country Director / Programme Executive, this is not a monitoring item — it is a medium-tier decision due within 6–18 months.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in sustainable development delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Country Director / Programme Executive answers for the outcome, not only the process.
The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a critical-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.

decision_options.rationale: Targeted reform (the recommendation as stated) scores highest (64%) among options that genuinely address the evidenced problem, under un-profile weighting, which weighs evidence strength and expected impact and feasibility and affordability and implementation speed and equity and sustainability and institutional readiness and political feasibility and risk and reversibility most heavily. decision_options.rationale: Targeted reform (the recommendation as stated) scores highest (64%) among options that genuinely address the evidenced problem, under un-profile weighting, which weighs evidence strength and expected impact and feasibility and affordability and implementation speed and equity and sustainability and institutional readiness and political feasibility and risk and reversibility most heavily. Maintaining the current approach scores higher on this weighted model — it is excluded from contention not by score but because it does not address the evidenced problem at all, which this scoring model treats as disqualifying rather than as one more weighted factor.

verdicts.rationale: Requires cabinet-paper readiness (government_intelligence) and full editorial consensus.
Requires a named Ministry-level owner and complete budget guidance.
Requires disclosed confidence intervals and a stated budget-implications position.
Requires a Leave No One Behind-aligned standard and a real regional breakdown.
Requires every one of the donor-evaluation dimensions present, not a majority.
Requires valid knowledge routing plus Research Editor and Statistician consensus.
Requires at least one recommendation genuinely classified as a Board Decision and full editorial consensus — not satisfied by publications that are not board-owned.
Requires valid knowledge routing, disclosed evidence quality, and Research Editor consensus.

quotes.quote: “Why does East Africa lag behind? Ask rural women, and the answer comes back to target trajectory.”
“Elsewhere, leave-no-one-behind gaps might be a footnote. For young adults in West Africa, it is the whole story.”
“Persons with disabilities in Southern Africa frame it simply: when financing alignment breaks down, everything else does too.”
“In Central Africa, data availability shapes whether frontline workers can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Low-income households in East Africa put it plainly: climate-development co-benefits are what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does West Africa lag behind? Ask service managers, and the answer comes back to target trajectory.”
“Elsewhere, leave-no-one-behind gaps might be a footnote. For community leaders in Southern Africa, it is the whole story.”
“Private-sector partners in Central Africa frame it simply: when financing alignment breaks down, everything else does too.”
“In East Africa, data availability shapes whether rural women can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Young adults in West Africa put it plainly: climate-development co-benefits are what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.

executive_book.executive_brief: What follows is a governed account of sustainable development performance in Africa: where it is working, where it is not, and what to do next. The evidence identifies uneven progress, concentrated risk, and a set of opportunities still open to act on. Every finding traces to a synthetic source record, every recommendation names an accountable owner, and every statistic in this publication is drawn from a single, internally consistent dataset.

evidence_book.finding: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.

What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Two things are true at once about data availability. The national picture is improving, and Central Africa — where frontline workers report a 86% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Sustainable development performance in Africa looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once data availability is disaggregated. Central Africa anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.

54%. That is the climate-development co-benefits signal recorded in East Africa, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, low-income households sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in East Africa, concentrated among low-income households. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

critical_findings.title: Target trajectory
critical_findings.text: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.
critical_findings.interpretation: This reading is more strongly supported by some evidence types than others — treat it as directionally reliable rather than precisely measured.
critical_findings.headline: Target trajectory is the binding constraint

critical_findings.title: Leave-no-one-behind gaps
critical_findings.text: What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
critical_findings.interpretation: Consistent across quantitative and qualitative sources, and most pronounced in lower-performing locations.
critical_findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the leave-no-one-behind gaps gap

critical_findings.title: Financing alignment
critical_findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
critical_findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
critical_findings.headline: Financing alignment gains remain exposed to reversal

critical_risks.risk: Leadership turnover during implementation stalls momentum on sustainable development
Evidence and monitoring capacity lags the pace of decisions being made on sustainable development

top_opportunities: Evidence-led reallocation of resources already committed to sustainable development
Scaling what is already working in the highest-performing region
Stronger feedback accountability between frontline delivery and leadership

executive_book.budget_implications: Most of the indicated action in sustainable development is fundable by reprioritizing existing lines; the highest-tier decisions may still need a dedicated allocation.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: Delaying action on sustainable development performance is unlikely to be neutral: the evidence suggests the current gap compounds rather than holds steady.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence in this synthetic demonstration is strong throughout; still not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: A 12–18 month improvement path for sustainable development is realistic, provided named owners and quarterly evidence review stay in place.
key_messages: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.

What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Two things are true at once about data availability. The national picture is improving, and Central Africa — where frontline workers report a 86% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Sustainable development performance in Africa looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once data availability is disaggregated. Central Africa anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.

54%. That is the climate-development co-benefits signal recorded in East Africa, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, low-income households sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in East Africa, concentrated among low-income households. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

findings.title: Target trajectory
findings.text: Progress on target trajectory remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in East Africa weakens after disaggregation, particularly for rural women, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is target trajectory in East Africa, where rural women sit furthest from parity.
findings.interpretation: This reading is more strongly supported by some evidence types than others — treat it as directionally reliable rather than precisely measured.
findings.headline: Target trajectory is the binding constraint

findings.title: Leave-no-one-behind gaps
findings.text: What would it take to fix leave-no-one-behind gaps? Start with West Africa, where the signal sits at 84% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. West Africa shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
findings.interpretation: Consistent across quantitative and qualitative sources, and most pronounced in lower-performing locations.
findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the leave-no-one-behind gaps gap

findings.title: Financing alignment
findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Southern Africa records a 85% signal for financing alignment; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Africa, financing alignment shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
findings.headline: Financing alignment gains remain exposed to reversal

findings.title: Data availability
findings.text: Two things are true at once about data availability. The national picture is improving, and Central Africa — where frontline workers report a 86% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Sustainable development performance in Africa looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once data availability is disaggregated. Central Africa anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.
findings.interpretation: Confirmed by both statistical and narrative evidence, with the sharpest signal in the weakest-performing areas.
findings.headline: Data availability now requires named accountability

findings.title: Climate-development co-benefits
findings.text: 54%. That is the climate-development co-benefits signal recorded in East Africa, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, low-income households sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in East Africa, concentrated among low-income households. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
findings.interpretation: Holds across independent evidence streams and intensifies in already under-resourced locations.
findings.headline: The climate-development co-benefits opportunity is actionable

editors.reason: Every recommendation traces to real evidence.
Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

commonMisconceptions: That an on-track national indicator implies no group is left behind
That contribution to an SDG target implies direct attribution to one programme
operationalRisks: Priority national data gaps undermining disaggregated reporting
Financing that is not tagged to SDG contribution logic
typicalRecommendationThemes: Focus acceleration plans on off-track targets
Fund disaggregated Leave No One Behind measures
Align budget tagging with SDG contribution logic

executive_commentary.text: For Country Director / Programme Executive, this is not a monitoring item — it is a medium-tier decision due within 6–18 months.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in sustainable development delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Country Director / Programme Executive answers for the outcome, not only the process.
The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a critical-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whatChanged: Regional performance gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

decision_options.rationale: Targeted reform (the recommendation as stated) scores highest (64%) among options that genuinely address the evidenced problem, under un-profile weighting, which weighs evidence strength and expected impact and feasibility and affordability and implementation speed and equity and sustainability and institutional readiness and political feasibility and risk and reversibility most heavily. Maintaining the current approach scores higher on this weighted model — it is excluded from contention not by score but because it does not address the evidenced problem at all, which this scoring model treats as disqualifying rather than as one more weighted factor.

verdicts.rationale: Requires cabinet-paper readiness (government_intelligence) and full editorial consensus.
Requires a named Ministry-level owner and complete budget guidance.
Requires disclosed confidence intervals and a stated budget-implications position.
Requires a Leave No One Behind-aligned standard and a real regional breakdown.
Requires every one of the donor-evaluation dimensions present, not a majority.
Requires valid knowledge routing plus Research Editor and Statistician consensus.
Requires at least one recommendation genuinely classified as a Board Decision and full editorial consensus — not satisfied by publications that are not board-owned.
Requires valid knowledge routing, disclosed evidence quality, and Research Editor consensus.

sdg_cards.title: No Poverty
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic multidimensional poverty measure stands at 77% against a 84% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.
